prompt: Which sentence demonstrates correct subject-verb agreement?

options.text: Neither the students nor the teacher was late.
options.because: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject closest to the verb. 'The teacher' (singular) is closest, so 'was' is correct.

options.text: Neither the students nor the teacher were late.
options.because: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the closest subject. 'The teacher' is singular, so 'was' is correct, not 'were'.

options.text: The team are playing well today.
options.because: In American English, collective nouns (team, committee, group) take singular verbs: 'The team is playing'. (British English may use plural.)

options.text: Everyone are invited to the party.
options.because: Indefinite pronouns (everyone, everyone, nobody, someone, something) are singular and take singular verbs: 'Everyone is invited'.

explanation: Subject-verb agreement rules: (1) singular subjects take singular verbs; (2) with neither...nor/either...or, the verb agrees with the nearest subject; (3) indefinite pronouns (everyone, nobody) are singular.

prompt: Write the correct form of the irregular verb.
template: The past simple of 'to go' is {{blank_1}}, and the past participle is {{blank_2}}.
explanation: 'Go' is one of the most irregular verbs in English: base form (go) → past simple (went) → past participle (gone). Note that 'went' and 'gone' come from completely different word roots.

prompt: The verb 'to be' has three different forms in the present tense: am, is, and are.
becauseTrue: Correct. 'To be' is the most irregular verb in English: I am, you are, he/she/it is, we/you/they are. In past tense it has two forms: was (I/he/she/it) and were (you/we/they).
becauseFalse: Incorrect. 'To be' does have three present forms: am (I), is (he/she/it), and are (you/we/they). This makes it the most irregular English verb.
